---
title: Fundamentals
description: >
  LightNet enables building websites in your community's language, supporting multiple languages, including right-to-left languages.
  Internationalization is a core feature, essential even if you're supporting just one language.
---

import { Aside } from "@astrojs/starlight/components";

LightNet lets you build websites in your community's language and supports multiple languages, including right-to-left languages.
Understanding LightNet's internationalization model is useful even if your site supports only one language.

## Two language layers

LightNet sites distinguish between two kinds of language. In many projects they are the same, but they serve different purposes:

- **Site language**: The language of the website interface, including button labels, menus, and page text.
- **Content language**: The language of a media item itself. For example, an English video has an English title and description.

Site languages are configured with `siteLanguages` in `astro.config.mjs`.
Language metadata such as display labels is stored separately in `src/content/languages/{bcp47}.json`.

## Configure site languages

This example configures English as the default site language and German as an additional locale:

```js title="astro.config.mjs"
import lightnet from "lightnet";
import { defineConfig } from "astro/config";

export default defineConfig({
  integrations: [
    lightnet({
      siteLanguages: [
        {
          code: "en",
          isDefault: true
        },
        {
          code: "de"
        }
      ],
    }),
  ],
});
```

Each configured site language should also have a matching language entry in `src/content/languages`.

For example, a corresponding English entry could look like this:


```json title="src/content/languages/en.json"
{
  "code": "en",
  "label": {
    "en": "English",
    "de": "Englisch"
  }
}
```

For step-by-step and reference details, see:

- [Add a site language](/build/i18n/add-language)
- [Languages reference](/content/languages)

## Route localization

Every page of a LightNet site is available in all configured site languages.
Routes are localized by prefixing the path with the configured locale code.

For example:

- `https://your.site/en/about`
- `https://your.site/de/about`

## Translation files and keys

LightNet uses translation keys that resolve to translated values based on the current locale.
Translation files live in `src/translations/`, with one file per site language named `[language-code].yml`.

For example:

```yml title="src/translations/en.yml"
# Optional description...
homepage.title: Welcome to my page!
```

You can use a translation key inside an Astro component like this:

```astro title="MyComponent.astro"
<h1>{Astro.locals.i18n.t("homepage.title")}</h1>
```

The `Astro.locals.i18n.t(...)` helper is available in all Astro files.
It resolves the key using the current locale derived from the page URL.

LightNet uses [i18next](https://www.i18next.com/) internally, so features such as plurals, interpolation, and context follow i18next conventions.

<Aside type="caution">
Enclose translation values in quotes if they contain special characters, such as `:`, `#`, or `-`.
</Aside>

- The translation key is on the left, and the translation value is on the right.
- Use periods (`.`) in keys to add context (e.g., `homepage.title`).

LightNet uses the same mechanism for built-in strings, for example the search page title (`ln.search.title`).
All built-in keys are defined in the [English translation file](https://github.com/LightNetDev/LightNet/blob/main/packages/lightnet/src/i18n/translations/en.yml) on GitHub.
Additional locale files in that folder are maintained by the community, and contributions are welcome.

By convention, LightNet's built-in translation keys start with `ln.`.
Your own project keys can use any other prefix, or no prefix at all, for example `homepage.title`.

### Translation resolution order

LightNet resolves translation keys in the following order:

1. Merge custom and built-in translations, with project translations taking precedence.
2. Check the translation file for the exact current locale.
3. Fallback to the base language (e.g., `en` for `en_US`).
4. Use any `fallback` locales defined for the current language in `siteLanguages`.
5. Fallback to the default site language.
6. Lastly, fallback to English translations.
7. If unresolved, `t(...)` throws an error because string inputs are treated strictly as translation keys.
